data.title: Consumer Sentiment Rises to 5-Month High as Inflation Concerns Ease, Survey Shows
data.description:
US consumer sentiment rose in July to the highest level in five months, while year-ahead inflation expectations dropped for a second month in a row, preliminary results from a University of Michigan survey showed Friday.

The main sentiment gauge climbed to 61.8 this month from 60.7 in June. The consensus was for a 61.5 print in a survey compiled by Bloomberg.

The gauge for current economic conditions ticked up 3.1% to 66.8, while the expectations measure increased 0.9% to 58.6, the survey results showed.

The latest report showed overall sentiment advancing for a second straight month, but it is 16% below the December 2024 level and "well below its historical average," according to Joanne Hsu, Surveys of Consumers director.

"Short-run business conditions improved about 8%, whereas expected personal finances fell back about 4%," Hsu said. "Consumers are unlikely to regain their confidence in the economy unless they feel assured that inflation is unlikely to worsen, for example if trade policy stabilizes for the foreseeable future."

Year-ahead inflation expectations fell to 4.4% from 5% last month, while the long-run outlook dropped for a third straight month, to 3.6% from 4%, the survey showed. Both readings were the lowest since February.

On Thursday, Federal Reserve Governor Adriana Kugler said inflation is likely to accelerate as the impact of tariffs on prices materializes. She favors holding interest rates steady "for some time."

"The interviews reveal little evidence that other policy developments, including the recent passage of the tax and spending bill, moved the needle much on consumer sentiment," Hsu said.

The tax and spending legislation, signed into law on July 4, prevents the end-of-year tax hike and expands the low-income housing tax credit, among other provisions.

data.title: Analysts on Chevron winning arbitration over Exxon in Hess deal
data.description:
Chevron closed its $55 billion acquisition of Hess on Friday after winning a landmark legal battle against larger rival Exxon Mobil to gain access to the biggest oil discovery in decades - the Stabroek Block off the coast of Guyana, which holds more than 11 billion barrels of oil.

STEWART GLICKMAN, DEPUTY RESEARCH DIRECTOR AT CFRA RESEARCH

"We see this transformative project driving meaningful production growth for at least the next three to five years, adding substantial new heft on the liquids side from Guyana, the Permian, and Gulf of Mexico. CVX and XOM must now put this legal dispute behind them as partners in Guyana, with the focus turning to advancing planned development stages."

ALLEN GOOD, ANALYST AT MORNINGSTAR

"We view the outcome positively for Chevron, given the quality and size of the Guyanese assets. We do not view the ruling as a negative for Exxon, whose ultimate goal seemed to be denying Chevron access or securing a favorable ruling for use elsewhere, rather than acquiring the assets itself."

AJAY PARMAR, OIL AND ENERGY TRANSITION ANALYTICS DIRECTOR AT CONSULTANCY ICIS

"Chevron's win in the arbitration over the Guyana oil assets case is a major coup for the U.S. oil major ... It strengthens Chevron's upstream presence in Guyana, Canada and U.S. on-shore ... We expect the company to continue to strengthen its portfolio over the coming years."

BIRAJ BORKHATARIA, ANALYST AT RBC CAPITAL MARKETS

"We expect (Chevron's) shares to outperform over the coming weeks... as investors have clarity on the investment case and can focus more on the free-cash flow inflection into 2026-27, as well as the new growth that comes with Hess assets."

"The synergy target is likely to be reviewed at the (Capital Market Day) on November 12, where Chevron expects to go deeper into its longer-term plans."

JASON GABELMAN, ANALYST AT TD COWEN

"Chevron had suggested when the deal was announced that 8 assets it highlighted of the joint entity (Guyana, GOA, Australia, TCO, Permian, DJ, Bakken, Eastern Med) are core. It is unclear if this still stands, with some likely seeing the Bakken as a divestment candidate."

"Chord Energy and Devon Energy have both been acquisitive in the basin, and we expect incremental interest on future opportunities either with Chevron as a consolidator or interest in divesting."

KIM FUSTIER, HEAD OF EUROPEAN OIL & GAS RESEARCH, HSBC

"Although the Hess acquisition is dilutive to Chevron's near-term per-share metrics given a high implied oil price (close to $80 a barrel), it is strategically important as it gives Chevron a significant growth runway beyond 2027 ... Hess propels Chevron from a 3.3 million barrels a day company to a 4.2 million bpd producer, not far off Exxon at 4.5 million bpd."

data.title: IGM Financial Maintained at Market Perform at BMO Ahead of Q2 Results; Price Target Raised to C$47.00
data.description:
BMO Capital Markets on Friday maintained its market-perform rating on the shares of IGM Financial while raising its price target on the wealth-management company to C$47.00 from C$46.00 ahead of its second-quarter results.

"Lowering Q2/25E EPS 1% to $1.02 (from $1.03) and 2025E EPS to $4.22 from $4.23 reflecting modestly lower advisory fee rates, higher asset-based compensation/lower forecast earnings from strategic investments, partially offset by positive impact of higher average and ending Q2/25 assets. 2026E EPS increases 1% as the impact of higher Q2/25 assets outweighs these other aforementioned items. Target price increases accordingly to $47 from $46. Remain Market Perform on valuation (trading at 10.4x forward EPS, generally in line with 10.6x five-year average), uncertain markets/strategic disadvantages versus banks," analyst Tom MacKinnon wrote.

data.title: The stock market's summer rally is going strong. But here's what can happen next.
data.description:
By Joseph Adinolfi

Stocks are approaching what is on average the weakest period for returns of the entire calendar year

There's an old adage in the stock market that investors should "sell in May and go away."

But doing that would have been a particularly big mistake this year.

Since hitting a 52-week closing low on April 8, the S&P 500 index SPX has rallied back by more than 25%, according to FactSet data. On Thursday, the index tallied its latest record closing high, and it even briefly tapped a fresh intraday record Friday shortly after the opening bell. It stood at 6,299 in recent trade on Friday.

While seasonal trends aren't destiny, they can provide a helpful road map for investors. This is why Jay Kaeppel of SentimenTrader says investors should be extra-careful over the next few months.

Kaeppel warned the traditional "summer rally" period is nearing its end, in a report shared with MarketWatch this week. What follows, on average, is the weakest period for stock-market returns of the entire calendar year. During an average year, weakness begins in late July, and typically persists through mid-October.

That doesn't mean weak returns are guaranteed. But after such a powerful comeback, investors should probably consider taking some profits.

Because when an overbought market collides with unfavorable seasonal trends, a short-term shakeout could follow.

"So, is the stock market 'doomed' to flounder and decline during this period? Not at all. Results can vary greatly year-to-year. Still, the main point is that investors who have become strongly bullish during the post-April sell-off lows may be wise to temper their enthusiasm for a while," Kaeppel said.

He is hardly the only stock-market strategist advising investors to proceed with caution here. On Thursday, BTIG's Jonathan Krinsky warned that the market was looking pretty frothy, as stocks benefiting from heavy social-media buzz have soared. Unprofitable tech stocks have also staged a huge comeback. Krinsky's conclusion? Expect a pullback some time in the not-too-distant future.

Scott Rubner, a former Goldman Sachs tactical strategist now at Citadel Securities, advised earlier this week that stocks could hit a rough patch in September, as the tailwind from corporate buybacks and trend-following funds starts to weaken.

U.S. stocks were treading water on Friday, with the S&P 500 SPX and Nasdaq Composite COMP marginally higher after touching fresh intraday records shortly after the opening bell. The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA was softer on the day.

-Joseph Adinolfi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

18 July 2025

UIL LIMITED

(LEI Number: 213800CTZ7TEIE7YM468)

Transaction in own shares and total voting rights

The Board of UIL Limited (the "Company") announces that on 18 July 2025 the Company purchased for cancellation 50,000 ordinary shares of 10p each at a price of 127.00p per share. The lowest/highest price paid per share was 127.00p/127.00p respectively.

Therefore, the total number of ordinary shares with voting rights in issue in the Company is now 92,638,602.

The above figure (92,638,602) may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the FCA's Disclosure and Transparency Rules.

data.title: Copper Holds Near Record High
data.description:
Copper futures in the US were at $5.55 per pound on Friday, holding the surge that topped at $5.7 on July 8th when US President Trump announced the US would place 50% tariffs on copper.

The tariffs were set to be enforced on August 1st shortly after, with Trump citing the aim to consolidate the copper industry instead of relying on imports of refined product.

The tariff will be enforced August 1st, even though new capacity takes years to be developed.

This drove the premium between US copper and comparable futures at the LME to a record of 25% as foreign benchmarks declined, signaling the end of increasing domestic stockpiles as traders moved copper into the US since tariff threats on base metals started in February.

If implemented, the tariff would threaten the domestic supply as the US imports nearly half of its consumption, with Chile as its primary supplier.

Refining and smelting capacity would likely be stressed, with only two operating smelters in the country.

data.title: Prosus offers to slash Delivery Hero stake to address EU concerns about Just Eat deal
data.description:
Prosus has offered to slash its Delivery Hero stake to below 10% and give up its board seat to address EU concerns over its 4.1 billion euro ($4.78 billion) Just Eat Takeaway deal, a person with direct knowledge of the matter said on Friday.

Prosus, which is majority owned by South Africa's Naspers , earlier said it had offered remedies to the European Commission but did not provide details.

The company's assurances to the Commission that it does not control nor influence the German meal delivery company Delivery Hero despite its 27.4% stake failed to convince the EU competition enforcer, people with direct knowledge of the matter said.

Delivery Hero and Just Eat Takeaway compete with each other in Austria, Bulgaria, Italy, Poland and Spain.

Delivery Hero and its Spanish unit Glovo were fined 329 million euros by the EU antitrust watchdog last month for taking part in a cartel which included an agreement not to poach each other's employees.

"We have submitted remedies that directly and comprehensively address the concerns expressed in their preliminary assessment, with a view to obtaining a Phase 1 approval," Prosus said in a statement

($1 = 0.8585 euros)

data.title: Camarico Announces Results of Annual General Meeting Held July 17, 2025
data.description:
(TheNewswire)

    July 18, 2025 – TheNewswire -Camarico Investment Group Ltd. (CSE:CIG) (“Camarico” or the“Company”) reports that at its annual general meeting (the"AGM") held on July 17, 2025, Camarico's Shareholdersapproved by majority: to set the number of directors at three (3), toelect all three directors standing for election; to re-appoint Mao& Ying LLP as the Company's auditor; and to approve the StockOption Plan.

data.title: TTF Prices Fall to 2-Week Low
data.description:
European natural gas futures dropped over 5% to below €34/MWh this week, driven by stronger supply from Norway, forecasts for increased wind power in Germany, and easing Asian demand.

Norwegian exports surged to 317 mcm/day after key outages at Nyhamna and Kollsnes were resolved, boosting available supply.

In Germany, higher wind output expected next week will reduce gas use for power generation.

Meanwhile, a recent heatwave in Northeast Asia had caused a spike in LNG demand, temporarily redirecting cargoes from Europe.

But signs of cooler weather and stabilizing imports—especially in Japan, South Korea, and China—have started easing global gas market pressures.

Some Asian prices jumped early in the week but have since pulled back.

The EU’s more flexible winter gas storage rules have also helped reduce competition with Asia.

Overall, conditions suggest the gas market may be returning to a more balanced, less volatile state after years of summer disruptions.
